title: Micro-Hydropower Generation from Waste Water of a Hydraulic Ram Pump
summary: Explored a dual-purpose rural water-and-energy system by modifying a hydraulic ram pump to drive a Pelton turbine and evaluating the design through Python flow simulations.
highlights: Designed a modified hydram with a Pelton turbine setup and achieved 63.54 percent D'Aubuisson efficiency in 2-D Python flow simulations.
Estimated 25 to 30 W of micro-hydropower from wastewater flow under different penstock lengths, nozzle diameters, and flow rates.
Positioned the work as a dual-purpose system for water delivery and decentralized energy access.

sections.heading: Problem framing
body: The project focused on whether a hydraulic ram pump could be extended beyond water delivery into a small but meaningful energy-generation system. The goal was to use wastewater from the pumping process instead of treating it as a pure loss.
That framing made the work less about a single device and more about a dual-purpose infrastructure concept for water and energy access in constrained settings.

sections.heading: System concept
body: The proposed configuration paired a modified hydram with a Pelton turbine so the discharge stream could be redirected into useful power generation. That required thinking about the hydraulic behavior of the system as an integrated flow-and-conversion problem rather than as two separate devices.
The design work centered on the practical interaction between head, nozzle geometry, and the turbine-facing side of the system.

sections.heading: Simulation and parameter study
body: A 2-D Python simulation framework was used to study the effect of penstock length, nozzle diameter, and flow-rate combinations on the system. The reported D'Aubuisson efficiency reached 63.54 percent under the modeled setup.
The simulations were used to estimate a micro-hydropower range of roughly 25 to 30 W, which gave the project a clear feasibility window instead of a purely conceptual outcome.

sections.heading: Why it matters
body: The value of the project was in showing how wastewater from a mechanical pumping system could become a second output rather than a discarded byproduct. That systems view fits the larger theme of extracting more utility from constrained infrastructure.
It also created a strong foundation for future work involving physical prototyping, optimization of losses, and field-oriented operating assumptions.
